video_title: What dangers does the US and Israel attack on Iran pose? | Inside Story
text:
Europe can no longer afford strategic ambiguity. It must take a clear, principled, and uncompromising stance in support of the Iranian people and their struggle to dismantle a theocratic regime that has governed through coercion, censorship, and fear. The bravery displayed by Iranian protesters, many of whom have paid the ultimate price in recent months, stands as one of the defining freedom movements of this generation. Their sacrifice is not a regional matter, it is a moral test for the democratic world. Silence is not neutrality, it is complicity.

The collapse of a clerical autocracy in Tehran would not merely shift internal power structures, it would reverberate across the Middle East. It would weaken the axis of ideological militancy, disrupt entrenched proxy networks, and redraw the strategic map from the Persian Gulf to the Mediterranean. Such a transformation would send a clear message that regimes rooted in religious absolutism and political repression are not immune to history.

There are competing philosophies of global order. One insists that stability must be preserved at almost any cost, even if that means tolerating authoritarian rule. The other argues that entrenched systems sometimes require confrontation to break cycles of stagnation and fear. Political figures such as Donald Trump have personified that disruptive doctrine, polarizing, unpredictable, and openly combative. To some, this is reckless volatility. To others, it is a necessary catalyst for structural change. History often shows that renewal is rarely born from comfort, but from rupture.

In this context, the geopolitical stakes surrounding Iran extend far beyond a single regime. They touch energy corridors, regional alliances, ideological influence, and the long arc of democratic legitimacy in the 21st century. When measured against the potential systemic impact of transformation inside Iran, even the war in Ukraine may ultimately represent one front within a broader and deeper global realignment.
